tata.FY2023-24: AMBIGUOUS: FY2022-23 is consolidated while FY2023-24 is not like-for-like

sections.about: This metric measures women employees as a percentage of total employees. It falls under BRSR Section A — General Disclosures, Employees; it is not an Essential or Leadership Indicator under the principle-wise section. A higher value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Tata leads throughout the three-year period: 8.43% in FY2022-23, 8.36% in FY2023-24 and 8.90% in FY2024-25. JSW improves steadily from 5.78% to 6.71%, SAIL is broadly flat at 6.11% → 6.29% → 6.28%, and Jindal Stainless improves from a low base of 2.89% to 4.41%. The structural constraint across the peer set is the heavy-industry operating model: mining, coke ovens, sinter, blast furnaces, BOF shops, slab casters, hot strip mills and maintenance shops historically have lower female participation than corporate, R&D, digital, procurement and commercial roles. Jindal’s FY2024-25 BRSR separately reports Board/KMP women representation and its employee diversity improvement, reinforcing that the company is improving from a low base.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2024-25, Tata ranks 1st at 8.90%; JSW is closest at 6.71%, which is 24.6% behind Tata. Tata’s rank has held steady at rank 1 across all three years, though FY2022-23 is consolidated and should not be treated as fully comparable. The FY2024-25 improvement is directionally positive and suggests Tata’s inclusion pipeline is stronger than peers, but absolute female participation remains low for a senior management lens.
sections.targets: No targets identified for this metric.
sections.comparabilityNotes: Tata FY2022-23 is consolidated; the later Tata values are not directly comparable with that year.
